coredata.dc:title: A fluorescence turn-down-up detection of Cu2+ and pesticide quinalphos using carbon quantum dot integrated UiO-66-NH2
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Bera, Mihir K.
Behera, Lingaraj
Mohapatra, Sasmita
coredata.dc:description: Synthesis of a fluorescent probe consisting of carbon quantum dot integrated metal–organic framework (OPCD@UiO-66-NH2) via a facile one-pot route has been reported. Carbon dot derived from orthophenylenediamine (OPCD) with a PLQY (26%) has been deposited on UiO-type metal organic framework by introducing OPCD during the synthesis of MOF. The resulting fluorescent OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 composite shows better fluorescence properties as well as photostability than pristine OPCD. The molecular interaction between UiO-66-NH2 and Cu2+ can be sensed by encapsulated OPCD. As a result the OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 shows remarkable fluorescence quenching on addition of Cu2+. However, in presence of organophosphorus pesticide quinalphos, the fluorescence intensity (at λ425) of CD@UIO-66-NH2 is regained because of competitive binding of Cu2+ with quinalphos and releasing OPCD@UIO-66-NH2 to the medium. Thus a dual sensor for Cu2+ and quinalphos can be established by measuring fluorescence intensity at λ425. The relative increase in fluorescence intensity for quinalphos (F/F0) works linearly in the concentration range 0–16 μM and the limit of detection is found to be 0.3 nM. The specific and selective recognition of our probe towards organophosphorus pesticide quinalphos makes it suitable for real field applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Carbon quantum dot
Pesticide
Quinalphos
Energy transfer

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of quinalphos based on “turn-off–on” FL of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2
Optimization of pH and response time

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic presentation of synthesis of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 and illustration of quinalphos sensing mechanism.
PXRD patterns of UiO-66-NH2 and the OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 composite.
(a) TEM image of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 b) OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 at higher magnification (c) and (d) HRTEM of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2.
(a) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms at 77 K for UiO-66-NH2 and the amine-OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 composite (b) Pore size distribution curve of UiO-66-NH2 and OPCD@UiO-66-NH2.
FTIR spectra of (a) OPCD (b) UiO-66, (c) UiO-66-NH2, (d) OPCD@UiO-66-NH2.
(a) Comparison of FL property of OPCD, (b)UiO-66-NH2, and (c) OPCD@UiO-66-NH2.

a) FL emission spectra of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 in the presence of different concentrations of copper ions b) Stern-Volmer’s plot showing linear range of detection, (c) FL response of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 in presence of different metal ions, (d) Change in FL decay lifetime of OPCD after addition of Cu2+.

(a) FL response of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2–Cu2+ probe system in the presence of different concentrations of quinalphos (0.6 nM-15 µM) at λex = 360 nm. b) Linear range of intensity enhancement and quinalphos concentration.
(a) XPS survey scan of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2-Cu2+ high resolution scan of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, (d) O1s, (e) Cu2p, (f) Zr3d.
(a) Effect of pH on sensing, (b) time on sensing.
(a) Selective FL enhancement of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 probe towards quinalphos in comparison to other pesticides (b) response in presence of other possible interfering species.

head.ce:title: A fluorescence turn-down-up detection of Cu2+ and pesticide quinalphos using carbon quantum dot integrated UiO-66-NH2

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology, Rourkela, Odisha 769008, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology, Rourkela, Odisha, INDIA-769008

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of a fluorescent probe consisting of carbon quantum dot integrated metal–organic framework (OPCD@UiO-66-NH2) via a facile one-pot route has been reported. Carbon dot derived from orthophenylenediamine (OPCD) with a PLQY (26%) has been deposited on UiO-type metal organic framework by introducing OPCD during the synthesis of MOF. The resulting fluorescent OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 composite shows better fluorescence properties as well as photostability than pristine OPCD. The molecular interaction between UiO-66-NH2 and Cu2+ can be sensed by encapsulated OPCD. As a result the OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 shows remarkable fluorescence quenching on addition of Cu2+. However, in presence of organophosphorus pesticide quinalphos, the fluorescence intensity (at λ425) of CD@UIO-66-NH2 is regained because of competitive binding of Cu2+ with quinalphos and releasing OPCD@UIO-66-NH2 to the medium. Thus a dual sensor for Cu2+ and quinalphos can be established by measuring fluorescence intensity at λ425. The relative increase in fluorescence intensity for quinalphos (F/F0) works linearly in the concentration range 0–16 μM and the limit of detection is found to be 0.3 nM. The specific and selective recognition of our probe towards organophosphorus pesticide quinalphos makes it suitable for real field applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphorus pesticides (OPP) account for over 38% of the total pesticides used globally due to their broad spectrum insecticidal activity, and reasonably low persistence in the environment [1,2]. OPP works by reducing the function of carboxylic ester hydrolases, such as acetylcholinesterase (AChE), chymotrypsin, hepatic carboxylesterases (aliesterases), plasma or butyril cholinesterase (BuChE), paraoxonases (asterases), and other nonspecific esterases inside the body [3,4]. Quinalphos (O,O-diethyl-O-quinoxalinyl phosphorothioate), a commonly used OPP, is applied to manage pests over a wide variety of crops such as rice, tea, cotton, and citrus fruits etc. [5]. Prolonged exposure to QP seriously affects central nervous system via intoxication through inhalation, ingestion or skin absorption [6]. In addition, QP affects the blood and brain through esterase activity [7]. World Health Organization (WHO) has set maximum residue limits 0.01 mg/kg (0.035 M) for quinalphos in vegetable, fruit and grain samples. Several analytical techniques such as spectrofluorometric [8–10], gas chromatography (GC) [11], enzyme-linked UV–Vis spectroscopy [12], high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry/mass spectrometry and gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) (HPLC–MS/MS) [13] have been reported in order to detect and quantify QP and other OPP in various samples. However, the application of these methods is limited due to the involvement of expensive equipment, tedious sample pre-treatment, and a drawn-out assay procedure. Considering these facts and realising the benefits of fluorescence (FL) detection method we aim to fabricate a nanomaterial-based FL sensor for the selective recognition of quinalphos in aqueous medium.

Fluorescence emission is a defining property of carbon dot (CD) which makes it an attractive probe in FL sensing [14]. In particular, introduction of CDs in different solid host materials, such as PVA, zeolite and metal–organic frameworks has provided CDs with interesting FL properties and more FL stability in solution [15]. The quantum dots-in-matrix strategy provides a unique platform for the development of this CD based sensors with improved optical properties, and further simplifies in real applications. Among different host materials explored so far for CDs, metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) have appeared as a unique class of favourable matrix material to load guest species [16–18]. More specifically luminescencent CD@MOF with inherent FL features of CD combined with the porous nature of MOFs can transform the host–guest molecular recognition into detectable changes in FL [19–22]. Such fluorescent MOFs are blessed with high loading capacity as a result of large surface area, well-regulated pore size, tunable emission properties and predictable nanostructure which make them an attractive sensory material [23–26]. However, often the as prepared MOF cannot meet the desired functions aiming at particular applications. So development of functional MOFs by judicious selection of metal, organic ligand and guest species has become extremely important in order to detect desirable analytes. Recently, zirconium- metal–organic frameworks (Zr-MOFs) has drawn considerable attention because of their outstanding stabilities in comparison to other MOFs [27–29]. UiO-66, a tough Zr-containing MOF composed of hexameric Zr6O32 units linked by terephthalic acid, has been extensively investigated for water splitting, luminescence sensing and photocatalysis [30,31]. Motivated by these facts, we feel that there remains ample opportunity to explore carbon dot incorporated UiO-66 as a sensory material for selective and sensitive detection of toxic organophosphorus pesticides.

In the present work, we establish a facile strategy to immobilize carbon dots synthesized from orthophenylenediammine in the channels of UiO-66-NH2 to develop luminescent OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 composite. We have prepared fluorescent OPCD from orthophenylenediammine through a hydrothermal method and then introduced it in UiO-66-NH2 through in-situ modification technique. The developed OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 offered excellent luminescence performance CDs as well as porous nature of UiO-66 and showed stable FL in aqueous solution. In presence of Cu2+ the FL intensity OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 is quenched because of formation of non-fluorescent ground state complex (OPCD@UiO-66-NH2-Cu2+). However, in presence of quinalphos OPCD@UiO-66-NH2-Cu2+is disturbed and FL of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 is regained thus establishing a reliable strategy for QP sensing.

Quinalphos, glyphosate, chloropyrifos, dichlorovos, marathion o-phenylenediamine(OPD), were procured from Sigma Aldrich. Rice and tomato were purchased from local market. Dichloromethane was purified by distillation over phosphorous pentoxide. All other chemicals were used as received without further purification. Millipore water (18.2 M) was used throughout the experiment.

In a typical experiment, ZrCl4 (0.169 g, 0.73 mmol) and the linker precursor 2-amino terephthalic acid (0.132 g, 0.73 mmol) were dissolved in 38 ml DMF by ultrasonication (1 min) in a 50 ml conical flasks properly topped with a Teflon cap. 2.1 ml of acetic acid was then added into the solution and dispersed by ultrasound for about 1 min. These tightly covered conical flasks were kept in an oven at 120 °C for 24 h. The while solids thus produced were isolated by centrifugation after cooled down to room temperature. To remove unreacted precursors, the solids were washed with DMF and then solvent was exchanged with acetone for six times over three days.
The synthesis and isolation of carbon dot from orthophenylenediamine following hydrothermal method has been described in our previous paper [32].

UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles were synthesized as described in Section 2.2.1 in the presence of variable concentration (500 µg to 1 mg/ml) of 1 ml of OPCD. The precipitates thus formed were washed with deionized water under ultrasonication for four times, followed by centrifugation. For activation the products were kept in a vacuum over for 5 h at 100 °C.

All FL spectra were obtained on a Horiba spectrophotometer equipped with a quartz cell with excitation and emission slit width 5 nm, excitation wavelength 365 nm. Typically, 50 µL of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 (0.1 mg/ml) was taken in 3 ml water. A calculated amount of Cu2+ was added into it and incubated for 5 min. In order to conduct FL sensing experiment, quinalphos of different concentrations (o to 15 μM) was added into 3.0 ml of probe solution and incubated for 15 min before measuring fluorescence.

The crystallographic states of the materials were studied using an X-ray diffractometer (Expert Pro, Phillips). The specific surface area of samples were calculated from N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms obtained at a liquid nitrogen temperature (77 K) using a surface area analyser (Quantachrome, US). Morphological and microstructural analysis were done using a high resolution transmission electron microscope (FEI Technai G2 30ST) operated at 300 kV. The presence of surface functional group was investigated through FTIR recorded by a spectrophotometer (IRAffinity-1S, Shimadzu). Surface composition was studied by analyzing XPS data using AlKα excitation source in PHI 5000 Versa Prob II (FEI Inc) instrument. All FL spectra have been recorded in a Horiba-PTI Quantamaster-400 spectrofluorometer.

To investigate method selectivity, some common potential interfering organophosphorus pesticides such as dichlorovos (DCV), glyphosate (GPH), marathion (MRT), chloropyrifos (CLP), sulphur containing pesticides such as diazinon (DIZ), S-methyl diethylthiocarbamate (SMEDEC), diethylthiocarbamate (DEC) were also tested at same concentration as that of quinalphos and similar experimental conditions described in section The selectivity of our OPCDs@UiO-66-NH2 sensing system towards quinalphos in presence of other possible pesticides and anions has been investigated taking dichlorovos, glyphosate, marathion, chloropyrifos, Br-, Cl-, F-, NO3 -, PO4 −3etc as interfering analytes following similar sensing protocol and maintaining concentration of quinalphos as 15 µM and concentration of interfering analyte at 15 µM. The pH of the sensing PBS medium was varied between 2 and 10 using 0.01HCl and 0.01 NaOH to determine the optimum pH to get a stable FL response against quinalphos.

To justify the feasibility of this sensor in practical applications, the sensing experiment was conducted in two different agricultural products taken from local market. For this purpose tomato and rice extract were spiked with variable amount of quinalphos (0.9, 1.4 and 1.9 µg/ml) solutions. In each case the calibration curve was plotted using standard quinalphos solution in the same sample extract. Recovery percentage was calculated using equation
Amount of QP from FL assay
Added amount of QP
× 100

The synthesis procedure for OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 is illustrated in Fig. 1. Green fluorescent OPCD were synthesized following a bottom-up approach from hydrothermal decomposition of o-phenylenediamine. The OPCD were obtained by carbonization of polymerized precursor followed by self-surface passivation process as described in our previous paper. UiO-66-NH2 was obtained by mixing DMF solution of ZrCl4, amino terephthalic acid (ATA), acetic acid. The powder XRD patterns of the UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles and OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 composites are revealing in Fig. 2. In the XRD pattern of UiO-66-NH2, the peaks at 7.4 and 8.5° corresponded to the crystal planes [111] and [200], respectively as reported in literature [33]. Both UiO-66-NH2 and CQD@UiO-66-NH2 matches with the simulated XRD pattern of UiO-66. There is no extra peak found validating that the pure crystalline phase with UiO-66 structure was successfully prepared via the present synthetic route. Even after increasing the carbon dot loading the composite frame work was not destroyed.

The morphology and surface composition of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 was investigated using scanning electronic microscope. The composite nanoparticles are spherical and size less than 200 nm (Fig. S1). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to examine the presence of carbon quantum dots in UiO-66-NH2 ( Fig. 3). TEM results also indicated that spherical OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 composite nanoparticles of size 40–45 nm have been formed with clearly visible pore channels (Fig. 3a). Further the encapsulated OPCQD in UiO-66-NH2 clearly shows spherical morphology with particle sizes of approximately 2–3 nm (Fig. 3(b) and (c)). The well-defined lattice fringes in the high resolution TEM shows crystalline nature of the incorporated carbon quantum dots. The deposition of OPCD in the pore channels of UiO-66-NH2 is further evidenced from N2 adsorption and desorption experiment at 77 K ( Fig. 4). The as prepared UiO-66-NH2 shows a BET surface area of 912 m2/g with an average pore volume of 1.349 cc/g and pore size 0.63 nm. However, after encapsulation of OPCD the surface area and pore volume have been reduced to 773. 46 m2/g and 0.521cc/g with a pore diameter 0.52 nm respectively in the OPCD@ UiO-66-NH2 sample. This decrease in surface area is possibly due to OPCD nanocrystals lined the pore channels of the OPCD@UiO-66 composites, resulting in a decreased micropore volume and channel diameter.

The surface chemistry of the UiO-66-NH2 and OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 composite were investigated by FTIR spectroscopy ( Fig. 5). The FTIR spectrum of OPCDs displays a characteristic broad band between 3050 and 3400 cm−1, indicating the presence of amino and oxygen functional groups on their surfaces. The characteristic bands at the wavenumbers 1576 and 1389 cm−1are attributed the stretching vibrations of νas(‒COO-) and νs(‒COO‒) of the carboxylate group in TPA. In case of synthesized UiO-66-NH2 an additional transmittance peak was observed at 1661 cm−1, which was attributed to C˭O stretching in DMF. The disappearance of transmittance peak around 1661 cm−1 in the spectrum of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 indicates that DMF molecules are replaced by the OPCD nanocrystals replaced and entered in the pore channels of the framework.

To explore the FL properties of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2, the FL emission intensity of OPCD, UiO-66, UiO-66-NH2, and OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 are compared ( Fig. 6a). It is observed that as such UiO-66 has negligible emission intensity at 450 nm when excited at 365 nm. But in case of UiO-66-NH2 there is slight increase in emission intensity possibly due to carbonization of organic ligand TPA. The contribution of lone pair of electrons from TPA probably enhances luminescence property to the UiO-66-NH2 network. As discussed in our previous paper, OPCD shows excitation dependent emission within the wavelength range (320–420 nm) with a maximum emission at λex 340 nm [30]. When excited at 365 nm, the emission bands of OPCD as well as OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 were observed at 425 nm demonstrating that the luminescence property of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 is originated from OPCD. The FL intensity of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 at 420 nm reaches the maximum value when the OPCDs volume is 150 µL which is the optimized amount of OPCD incorporated in the OPCD@UiO-66-NH2. Further, the luminescence and structural stability of the material has been further studied to evaluate the feasibility of the material as a fluorescent probe in the water medium. The FL intensities of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 probe in different pH values, different storage days and NaCl concentrations are observed. The FL intensity of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 hardly changed in the wide pH range 6–10 indicating the suitability of the probe in acidic to alkaline environment (Fig. 6b). At lower pH the –NH2 groups of OPCD as well as UiO-66-NH2 get protonated. Hence OPCD may get detached from UiO-66-NH2 matrix due to Coulombic repulsion. However in between pH 6–10 OPCD gets trapped in UiO-66-NH2 matrix due to possible hydrogen bonding. Hence the fluorescence property of OPCD is enhanced due to confinement of OPCD in the host matrix. To investigate stability of the fluorophore FL intensity of the dispersion was measured with respect to time for 7 days. After 4 days there is 10% reduction in the FL intensity due to settlement of agglomerated particles, but after that the FL intensity of the dispersion remains consistent signifying the suitability of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 for sensing applications (Fig. 6c). The stable FL of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 is a result of high dispersion stability of the fluorophore in aqueous medium which has been evidenced from measurement of hydrodynamic size of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 against time (Fig. S2, S3). The excellent stability of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 in aqueous medium is attributed to the deposition of OPCD on the surface of UiO-66-NH2. Furthermore, there is no significant change in peak intensity when the ionic strength was varied from 0.2 to 1.0 molL−1 which indicates that OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 shows excellent stability in high ionic strength solution (Fig. 6d). With stable FL along with good anti interference property we explored the applicability OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 FL sensor for detection of quinalphos.

With a number of excellent FL characteristics and availability of surface amine groups in OPCD@UiO-66-NH2, we examined the FL response of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 towards different concentration of Cu2+ ion at 25 °C ( Fig. 7a). Gradual addition of copper ion into the aqueous dispersion of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2, the emission peak at 420 nm was instantly decayed within 5 min. When the conc. of Cu2+ ion increased from 3.33 µM to 166.5 µM there was almost 75% drop in FL intensity of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2. This FL quenching process could be quantitatively described by the Stern-Volmer equation, F0/F= 1 +KSV[Q], where F0 and F are the FL intensities of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 suspension before and after the addition of Cu2+ ion, respectively. [Q] is the molar concentration of quinalphos, and KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. In the Cu2+concentration range 3.33–33.3 µM, SV plot exhibits a good linear correlation with R2 = 0.983 (Fig. 7b). The quenching constant of KSV is found to be 4.5 × 105 M−1 for Cu2+ ion. Under the optimized conditions at pH 7 the limit of detection (LOD) for copper was found to be 0.5 µM. To verify the specificity OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 fluorescent probe towards Cu2+, we tested the FL variations of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 in the presence of equivalent various possible coexistent cations Cr3+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+ (Fig. 7c). The differential quenching effects of metal ions with MOF is mainly associated with their electronic nature. Due to presence of unsaturated electronic states in copper ions could lead to nonradiative energy/charge transfer process from OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 to Cu2+. To gain further insight into quenching mechanism, the FL lifetime decay of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 was measured both in the presence and absence of Cu2+ ion at 25 °C. As shown in Fig. 7d the curve could be well fitted by single exponential lifetime decay. The excited state of the OPCD@UiO-66-NH2-Cu2+ (τ = 15.51 ns) decays faster than the free case (τ = 15.61 ns) suggesting that the FL quenching may occur through dynamic mechanism. Again the quenching of FL response OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 by Cu2+ ions may be attributed to the formation of cupric amine complexes at the surface of UiO-66-NH2. The pH value of the solution plays an important role in the sensing system. OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 has strong FL response towards Cu2+ in the range of pH 6–8.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of quinalphos based on “turn-off–on” FL of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2
ce:section.ce:para: The FL response of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 +Cu+2 probe against quinalphos has been investigated ( Fig. 8). With increasing the concentration of quinalphos there was a remarkable enhancement the emission intensity at 425 nm. At optimized reaction conditions the FL turn on response towards quinalphos is presented in Fig. 8a. The change in FL intensity of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 +Cu2+ probe with respect to quinalphos clearly shows a linear relationship in the concentration range 0–15 µM (Fig. 8b) with a linear regression equation is F/F0 = 3.1[QP] + 2.78. The limit of detection is found to be 0.3 nM, which is lowest among reported methods for detection of quinalphos (Table S1).

According to the UV–vis absorption spectra, the presence of Cu2+ caused a significant decrease in absorbance of the OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 suspension at 260 nm, and about 15 nm red shift, which could be attributed to the coordination between Cu2+ and -NH2 groups of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 [34,35], thus quenching the FL of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2. The complexation of Cu2+ through surface –NH2 groups is evidenced from XPS spectroscopy. As shown in figure the survey XPS spectrum of OPCD-UiO-66-NH2 Cu has five typical peaks at 284, 532, 399, 183, 932 eV corresponding to C1s, O1s, N1s, Zr3d and Cu2p respectively. The high resolution spectrum of C1s illustrated four different types carbon bonds present at 284.66, 285.83, 286.76 and 288.55 eV, which represented C‒C, C‒N, C‒O (alkoxy) and C˭O respectively ( Fig. 9a). The N1s spectrum shows a peak at 399.58 eV which reveals the presence of amino nitrogen which is originated from amino terephthalic acid (Fig. 9b). The high resolution O1s spectrum indicated two specific peaks at 531.71 and 532.04 eV, which represented the presence of Zr‒O and C‒O‒H functionality respectively. The Zr3d region consisted of two individual peaks at 182.81 (Zr3d5/2) and 185.04 (Zr3d3/2), which represented Zr-O bond signals. In addition, high resolution spectrum of Cu2p indicated two intense peaks at about 933 eV and 953 eV, along with two charge transfer shake ups at 944.29 and 963. 29 eV which corresponds to 2p binding energy of Cu2+ in a Cu2+-Amine complex as reported earlier in case of Cu2+-amine resin complex [36]. All these results represented the formation of non-radiative ground state complex formation between Cu2+ and OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 after addition of Cu2+ to OPCD@UiO-66-NH2. After addition of quinalphos there is remarkable enhancement in FL intensity which can be attributed to the following facts. Quinalphos contains functional groups such as quinoxalinyl and phosphorothioate which have been strong chelating ability towards Cu2+. Thus quinalphos formed chelated compound with Cu2+ which disturbs the nonfluorescent OPCD@UiO-66-NH2-Cu2+ complex and hence causes the recovery of FL intensity of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2. With the same concentration of OPCD, Cu2+ and quinalphos the turn-on sensing is much less in comparison to that of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2, Cu2+ and quinalphos. The porous nature of UiO-66-NH2 facilitates the adsorption of Cu2+ which ultimately promotes the interaction of quinalphos with the sensing probe. The porous nature of UiO-66-NH2 essentially plays a vital role in enhancing the sensitivity of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2-Cu2+ towards quinalphos (Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of pH and response time
ce:section.ce:para: The pH of the sensing medium plays an important role for spectrophotometric detection of quinalphos. As shown in Fig. 10, the FL turn on intensity of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 probe increases with increasing the pH value and had a maximum value at pH 7, then decreased as the pH increased above 7. The probable reason behind such behaviour is, at lower pH i.e. in acidic medium surface groups of the OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 were protonated and unable to coordinate with Cu2+ while in case of alkaline medium partial occurs hydrolysis of Cu2+ which would impede the coordination between Cu2+ and the functional groups on the surface of the OPCD@UiO-66-NH2. Thus pH 7 is the optimum for further study (Fig. 10a). We investigated the response time of the sensing process at constant pH 7. There was four times increase in the FL intensity of the probe in presence of 15 µM quinalphos after 40 mins incubation time (Fig. 10b).

We screened the FL response of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2-Cu2+ probe towards a series of organophosphorus pesticides such as dichlorovos (DCV), glyphosate (GPH), marathion (MRT), chloropyrifos (CLP), sulphur containing pesticides such as diazinon (DIZ), S-methyl diethylthiocarbamate (SMEDEC), diethylthiocarbamate (DEC) in order to verify the selectivity of the pesticide. The FL response (F/F0) of OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 towards quinalphos is much higher (>3.5) as compared to other pesticides ( Fig. 11a, Fig S6-S8). Further, the FL response of our probe towards quinalphos is also not affected in presence of other pesticides (Fig. 11b) which shows there is no interference from similar type of pesticides in the sensing process.

In practical study to justify the feasibility of this sensor, the sensing experiment was conducted in two different agricultural products taken from local market. The recovery percentage varies from 98% to 105% (Table S2) which shows that this FL sensor system has reasonable accuracy and can be utilized for on-field detection of quinalphos in practical samples.

In summary, a facile method for direct synthesis of CD@UiO-66-NH2 has been reported. The synthesized NH2-functionalized UiO-66 integrated the advantages of the FL features of OPCD and the microporosity characteristics of UiO-66 and thus exhibited an improved FL selectivity and sensitivity compared to the free OPCD. In the presence of Cu2+, the intense luminescence of CD@UiO-66-NH2 is quenched because of formation of non-fluorescencent CD@UiO-66-NH2-Cu2+ ground state complex. However, when quinalphos is added to the sensing medium the FL intensity is regained due to affinity of quinalphos towards Cu2+, thus achieving a reliable detection method for quinalphos. Due to easy operational method this technique can be applied for detection of traces of quinalphos in real samples.
Both MKB and LB have contributed towards experimental work.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has been financially supported by SERB, DST, Govt. of India (CRG/2018 /0001717). MKB and LB thank NIT Rourkela for providing fellowship for their doctoral research.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2021.126792.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Linker-assisted immunoassay and liquid chromatography/mass spectrometry for the analysis of glyphosate
Anal. Chem.
